title: Caspia Technologies is pioneering a new, agentic chip and system security approach at DAC 2026 - Semiwiki
gist: Caspia Technologies, a 2020-founded Gainesville, Florida startup, is bringing an agentic AI platform (CODAx) for static RTL security verification to DAC 2026, positioning silicon security verification as a mandatory design-flow step in response to AI-powered cyberattacks.

themes.theme: Agentic RTL security verification as a product category

insights.claim: Caspia Technologies is commercializing CODAx, a static RTL security enhancement tool that uses AI agents to let general engineering teams perform expert-level chip security verification without dedicated security specialists.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A new EDA sub-category (agentic silicon security) is emerging that could become a required signoff step, expanding the security-verification TAM within chip design flows.
insights.quote: Rather than relying on manual reviews or a small group of security specialists, CODAx enables engineering teams to continuously identify, analyze, and remediate security vulnerabilities throughout development.

insights.claim: Caspia's initial CODAx product focuses on static analysis of RTL designs, with a roadmap to expand into automated security-assertion generation for formal tools and system-level hardware/software security testing.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Signals the platform's expansion path from point tool toward full-flow security coverage, a directional read on where security-EDA spend is heading.

insights.claim: Caspia is adding agentic capabilities to CODAx including Asset Assist (automated identification of security assets) and Report Assist (accelerated violation triaging).
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Concrete feature set to benchmark against incumbent EDA security offerings from Synopsys/Cadence/Siemens.
insights.locator: You will also learn about the latest agentic security verification capabilities

themes.theme: AI-driven threat landscape as demand driver

insights.claim: AI-powered cyber tools are lowering the expertise barrier to discovering and exploiting hardware vulnerabilities, which Caspia argues makes RTL security verification a mandatory design step rather than an afterthought.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Frames a structural demand tailwind: if AI attackers scale, every complex chip design team becomes a buyer of automated security verification.
insights.quote: AI-powered cyber tools are already transforming the threat landscape. What once required highly specialized expertise can now be automated, accelerated, and scaled

insights.claim: Caspia performed a security analysis on a popular open-source root-of-trust design and will present its findings at DAC, implying discoverable weaknesses in widely reused security IP.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: If widely used open-source root-of-trust IP carries exploitable weaknesses, downstream chips embedding it inherit risk, strengthening the case for automated RTL security scanning.
insights.quote: We'll review the analysis we recently performed on a popular open-source root-of-trust design.
insights.locator: You will also learn about the latest agentic security verification
